title: Tournament vs Cash Game Strategy
subtitle: ICM, stack survival, and when chips change value
intro: In cash games, every chip has the same dollar value — winning or losing $100 always means the same thing. You can reload at any time, so decisions are driven by expected value (EV) in pure chip terms.

Tournaments change the math. Your stack represents survival and ladder equity, not just raw chips. Independent Chip Model (ICM) adjusts for pay jumps: folding can be correct even with a strong hand when calling risks busting near the money.

Short stacks in tournaments often shift to push-or-fold strategy, while deep stacks in cash games can play more speculative hands. Recognizing which format you are in — and how stage, antes, and pay structure affect risk — is essential for choosing the right lines.

questions.question: In a cash game, winning 1,000 chips is worth:
options: The same dollar value regardless of stack size
More when you are short stacked
Nothing until you leave the table

explanations.A: Correct — cash chips convert to dollars at a fixed rate.
explanations.B: Stack depth doesn't change chip dollar value in cash.
explanations.C: Blind size affects decisions, not chip redemption value.
explanations.D: Chips have value while you play and when you cash out.

remediation.question: Cash game chips differ from tournament chips because:
options: Cash chips always map to real money at a fixed rate
Tournament chips are worth more per chip always
Only tournament chips can be rebought

explanations.A: Correct — $1 in chips is $1 in value.
explanations.B: Cash chips don't expire mid-session.
explanations.C: Tournament chip value is non-linear near pay jumps.
explanations.D: Some cash games allow reloads; tournaments have set buy-ins.

remediation.question: Reloading in a cash game means:
options: You can add chips and keep playing at the same stakes
You must wait until the tournament ends
Your stack resets to starting tournament chips
All prior losses are erased from the deck

explanations.A: Correct — cash games allow buying more chips between hands.
explanations.B: Tournaments don't allow mid-event rebuys in freezeouts.
explanations.C: Tournament starting stacks are fixed per event.
explanations.D: The deck isn't reset by reloading.

questions.question: ICM (Independent Chip Model) primarily adjusts for:
options: Pay jumps — chips worth less when busting costs a payout

explanations.A: Correct — ICM converts chip EV into prize-pool equity.
explanations.B: Hand strength is separate from ICM.
explanations.C: Rake is a cash-game house fee, not ICM.

remediation.question: Near the bubble, ICM makes players:
options: More risk-averse to avoid busting before a pay jump
Play identical to deep cash games

explanations.A: Correct — survival equity rises near pay jumps.
explanations.B: ICM often tightens ranges, not widens to any two.
explanations.C: Pay structure is central to ICM.
explanations.D: Cash games lack tournament ladder equity.

remediation.question: ICM equity measures:
options: Your share of the remaining prize pool given stack sizes

explanations.A: Correct — stacks map to expected tournament payout.
explanations.B: Blind level affects play but isn't ICM itself.
explanations.C: Rake is unrelated to ICM math.

questions.question: On the tournament bubble (one player left to bust before money):
options: Medium stacks often tighten to survive into the money
Everyone should play loose to bust faster

explanations.A: Correct — medium stacks fear busting one spot from cashing.
explanations.B: Short stacks may shove; medium stacks usually don't play loose.
explanations.C: ICM is strongest on the bubble.

remediation.question: Big stacks on the bubble can:
options: Pressure medium stacks who can't call without busting

explanations.A: Correct — fold equity rises vs risk-averse medium stacks.
explanations.B: Big stacks exploit with aggression, not pure folding.
explanations.C: Steals are a key bubble weapon for chip leaders.

remediation.question: Short stacks on the bubble often:
options: Shove wider because waiting bleeds chips to blinds
Call off with any two cards regardless of ICM

explanations.A: Correct — blind pressure forces push-or-fold lines.
explanations.B: Pure folding guarantees blind death.
explanations.C: ICM still matters but survival pressure dominates.

questions.question: With 10 big blinds in a tournament, optimal strategy often shifts to:
options: Push-or-fold preflop — shove or fold, minimal flat calls

explanations.A: Correct — short stacks can't afford multi-street postflop play.
explanations.B: Limp-calling burns chips and invites isolation.
explanations.C: Aces should be played aggressively, not only slow played.
explanations.D: Deep cash strategy requires stack depth this hand lacks.

remediation.question: Push-or-fold charts depend on:
options: Whether it is a cash game always

explanations.A: Correct — position and antes widen or tighten shove ranges.
explanations.B: Suits matter slightly but stack and position dominate.
explanations.C: Push-or-fold is primarily a tournament short-stack tool.

remediation.question: At 8bb, flat-calling a raise is often bad because:
options: You commit too much of your stack without fold equity
Flat calls always win bigger pots

explanations.A: Correct — calling leaves a tiny stack with no leverage.
explanations.B: Flat calls are legal but usually -EV short.
explanations.C: Small stacks can't realize implied odds well.
explanations.D: Blinds are a major fraction of an 8bb stack.

questions.question: Tournament antes (everyone posts each hand) mainly:
options: Increase pot size and pressure short stacks to act

explanations.A: Correct — antes bloat pots and accelerate blind pressure.
explanations.B: Antes add to blinds, not replace them.
explanations.C: All active players post antes in most formats.
explanations.D: Antes are common in tournaments, rare in cash.

remediation.question: When antes kick in, steal ranges typically:
options: Widen because dead money in the pot rewards aggression

explanations.A: Correct — more dead money improves steal EV.
explanations.B: Steals use wider hands than aces only.
explanations.C: Steals become more important, not gone.
explanations.D: Steals are preflop/postflop opens, not river-only.

remediation.question: Antes affect short stacks by:
options: Adding another forced cost each hand beyond blinds

explanations.A: Correct — antes bleed short stacks faster.
explanations.B: Antes cost chips, they don't add stack.
explanations.C: Blind levels continue with antes added.
explanations.D: Pay jumps depend on eliminations, not antes alone.

questions.question: In cash games, the primary decision metric is usually:
options: Chip EV — maximize long-run dollar expected value
Ladder equity in the prize pool

explanations.A: Correct — each chip has fixed dollar value; play for +chip EV.
explanations.B: ICM is a tournament concept, not standard cash play.
explanations.C: Prize-pool ladders exist in tournaments, not cash.
explanations.D: Cash has no min-cash threshold like tournaments.

remediation.question: A +$50 EV call in cash means:
options: Profitable long run if repeated in similar spots
You must leave the table immediately

explanations.A: Correct — EV is an average over many trials.
explanations.B: EV is expected value, not a guaranteed outcome.
explanations.C: No table exit required for +EV plays.
explanations.D: ICM doesn't apply to cash chip EV.

remediation.question: Cash game bankroll management focuses on:
options: Having enough buy-ins to absorb variance at your stakes
Only playing when ICM favors you

explanations.A: Correct — variance requires adequate bankroll depth.
explanations.B: Min-cash is tournament language.
explanations.C: Reloads are normal in cash when bankroll allows.
explanations.D: ICM is irrelevant to cash bankroll rules.

questions.question: Tournament strategy sometimes sacrifices chip EV for:
options: Survival equity — staying alive to reach higher pay jumps

explanations.A: Correct — busting costs future prize-pool equity.
explanations.B: Showing cards isn't a strategic tradeoff.
explanations.C: Players don't choose rake amounts.
explanations.D: Dealer speed isn't a strategic goal.

remediation.question: Folding a marginal spot ITM (in the money) can be correct because:
options: Busting loses equity from higher pay rungs
Rules require folding on the river
Cash games use the same logic always

explanations.A: Correct — ladder equity can outweigh small chip EV edges.
explanations.B: Folding wins zero chips that hand.
explanations.C: No rule forces folds on the river.
explanations.D: Cash rarely folds for ladder reasons.

remediation.question: Chip EV vs ICM EV differs when:
options: Pay jumps make busting costlier than chips suggest
All stacks are equal depth in cash

explanations.A: Correct — ICM discounts chip EV near pay ladders.
explanations.B: Heads-up changes ranges but ICM still applies in MTTs.
explanations.C: Blinds are never zero in real play.
explanations.D: Equal stacks don't remove ICM in tournaments.

questions.question: Larger pay jumps (e.g., 5th to 4th place) generally make players:
options: Play identical to early tournament stages

explanations.A: Correct — bigger pay gaps increase cost of busting.
explanations.B: Light shoves increase bust risk, opposite of ICM pressure.
explanations.C: Stack sizes remain central to decisions.
explanations.D: Early stages have weaker ICM pressure.

remediation.question: Final-table pay jumps mean:
options: Each elimination can be worth a large equity swing
All places pay the same amount

explanations.A: Correct — top-heavy structures amplify ICM.
explanations.B: Payouts increase with higher finishes.
explanations.C: ICM is often strongest at final table.

remediation.question: Flat payout structures (small pay jumps) tend to:
options: Reduce ICM pressure vs top-heavy structures
Convert tournaments into cash games legally

explanations.A: Correct — smaller jumps mean less survival premium.
explanations.B: Folds still depend on spots, not automatic.
explanations.C: Blinds remain in all formats.
explanations.D: Format rules differ even if ICM is milder.

questions.question: Bubble: you have 15bb in MP with A♠ J♦. Chip-leader CO shoves 25bb. ICM-heavy field — best play often:
options: Fold — calling risks busting one spot from money despite a decent hand
Always call — AJ is too strong to fold ever
Re-raise to 5bb and fold to shove

explanations.A: Correct — ICM makes survival premium huge; AJ vs shove range can be a fold.
explanations.B: Chip EV call can be -ICM EV on the bubble.
explanations.C: Min-raise commits chips without resolving the spot.
explanations.D: Showing cards is not a legal strategic option.

remediation.question: ICM folding means:
options: Passing chip-EV edges to preserve prize-pool equity

explanations.A: Correct — survival can outweigh marginal chip EV.
explanations.B: Pairs still fold in bad ICM spots.
explanations.C: ICM is tournament-specific.

remediation.question: Vs chip-leader shove on bubble, medium stacks should:
options: Call wider because leader is bluffing always

explanations.A: Correct — busting costs massive equity near money.
explanations.B: Leaders shove merged and strong ranges, not air always.
explanations.C: First-in shoves differ from facing a shove.

questions.question: Final table: you are chip leader with 40bb. Three short stacks at 6–8bb. Best exploitative approach:
options: Open wide and pressure short stacks — they can't call without busting risk
Only play AA and fold everything else

explanations.A: Correct — big stack vs short stacks uses ICM fold equity aggressively.
explanations.B: Leaders widen to steal, not nit down to aces only.
explanations.C: Checking away leverage burns chip EV and ICM edge.

remediation.question: Chip-leader ICM advantage means:
options: Others fold more often, giving free chips
Leader must fold to any raise

explanations.A: Correct — fear of busting grants fold equity.
explanations.B: Leaders apply pressure, not auto-fold.
explanations.C: Short stacks call tighter, not wider.

remediation.question: Vs three short stacks, chip leader should avoid:
options: Unnecessary confrontations with other big stacks early

explanations.A: Correct — big-vs-big fights help short stacks ladder.
explanations.B: Steals are primary weapon vs shorts.
explanations.C: Position remains valuable.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Pay jumps are tournament.
explanations.C: Chips persist.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Suits minor.
explanations.C: Separate topic.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Not always loose.
explanations.C: ICM peaks.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Needs depth.
explanations.C: Too passive.

placementQuestions.question: Antes:
options: Add dead money and pressure shorts

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Add to blinds.
explanations.C: All players post.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Tournament idea.
explanations.C: Tournament tool.

placementQuestions.question: Tournaments may trade chip EV for:

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Not a tradeoff.
explanations.C: House sets rake.

placementQuestions.question: Chip leader vs short stacks:
options: Apply ICM pressure with wide opens

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Too tight.
explanations.C: Steals key.
